summary: I was born in Natick, Massachusetts on May 10th, 1947, but I didn't stay there very long. It was just the place of my birth before my family moved elsewhere. Natick marks the beginning of my life story, even though my upbringing happened in other places.

My mother, Mary Elizabeth Duffy, was born and raised in Wellesley, Massachusetts. She came from a warm and affectionate Irish background, which shaped much of my early family life. Wellesley represented a more refined community compared to where my dad grew up.

My father, Daniel J. Boudreau, was born and raised in East Boston, Massachusetts, which was an Italian community despite his French and Irish heritage. East Boston was where he grew up and shaped his early life before pursuing social work. It was quite different from my mother's community in Wellesley.

I attended Boston College after graduating high school in 1965, majoring in East European history. It was a family school—my father and uncles went there—and I wanted to follow in their footsteps. Although I didn't apply myself fully, Boston College was a good institution that provided me with a solid education.

My father was the executive director of Catholic Charities in Newburgh and Albany, New York. His career in social work deeply influenced my own path. Catholic Charities was part of the Catholic community that shaped my upbringing and family values.

I grew up in Newburgh, New York during the late 1950s and early 1960s, which was an ideal place to grow up back then. I attended Saint Mary's School there, taught by Dominican nuns. Although Newburgh has since fallen on hard times, my childhood memories of it are very positive.

My father also worked as the executive director of Catholic Charities in Albany, New York, after his time in Newburgh. Albany was part of his career in social work, which influenced my own decision to pursue social work later on. It was one of the places where my family lived during my early years.

I began my grade school education at Saint Mary's School in Newburgh, New York, where the Dominican nuns taught. It was a small Catholic school and a good place to start my education in the late 1950s. The school was part of my early Catholic upbringing.

The Dominican nuns were the teachers at Saint Mary's School in Newburgh where I went to grade school. Their presence was a significant part of my early Catholic education in the late 1950s and early 1960s. They helped shape the foundation of my faith and schooling.

Newburgh, where I grew up and attended school, is located on the Hudson River about 60 miles north of New York City. The river was part of the landscape of my childhood and the community around me. It was an ideal environment for a kid growing up in the late 50s and early 60s.

I attended Saint Patrick's High School, a small Catholic school, for three of my four high school years. It was an all-boys school run by the Christian Brothers, known for their strict discipline. I left after three years because my mother didn't like the physical discipline used there.

James Patterson was a classmate and my best friend during my sophomore and junior years at Saint Patrick's High School. Even then, he was an avid reader and seemed more mature and focused than the rest of us. He has since become a prolific author, even co-writing a book with Bill Clinton.

Bill Clinton co-authored a book with James Patterson, who was my high school friend and a prolific author. While I didn't know Clinton personally, his connection to Patterson is something I find interesting given my friendship with James. It highlights the success of people I knew from my youth.

The Christian Brothers ran Saint Patrick's High School, where I attended for three years. They were known for their use of physical discipline, which my mother strongly opposed. This was the main reason I left the Catholic school for public school in my senior year.

After graduating from Boston College, I volunteered for Vista, which was like a domestic Peace Corps, for about a year and two months in Central Virginia. I worked with the National Welfare Rights Organization and helped organize welfare recipients. It was a maturing experience and one of the best of my life.

During my time as a Vista volunteer in Central Virginia, I worked with the National Welfare Rights Organization to help welfare recipients gain access to benefits. The local authorities were refusing to put people on welfare rolls, so we organized to address that. It was part of my early social work experience.

While volunteering with Vista in Central Virginia, we collaborated with the University of Virginia to bring a medical clinic to Buckingham County. This partnership was part of our efforts to improve local health services. It was a meaningful part of my work during that time.

I spent my Vista volunteer year working in Buckingham County, Virginia, where we fought to get welfare recipients recognized and helped establish a medical clinic. It was a rural area with significant challenges, but the experience helped me mature and shaped my future career. I still keep in touch with people I met there.

After my Vista service, I went to graduate school at Rutgers University and earned a master's degree in social work. Both my parents were social workers, so it was a natural path for me. It was also where I met my future wife, Faith Santangelo, during a group dynamics class.

I met Faith Santangelo two months into graduate school at Rutgers in a group dynamics class when we struck up a conversation in an elevator. She was a Sister of Mercy and had taken temporary vows as a nun before leaving the convent. We knew from that first meeting that we were meant to be together, and we've been married ever since.

Faith Santangelo, whom I married, was a member of the Sisters of Mercy and had taken temporary vows as a nun for four and a half years before leaving. Her background as a Sister of Mercy was part of her story when we met at Rutgers. It was a unique aspect of our relationship and her personal journey.

About years ago, Faith was diagnosed with stage 4 lymphoma that affected 40% of her bone marrow. It was a very serious diagnosis, and we feared losing her. We sought treatment at MD Anderson Cancer Center where a relatively new protocol was available, which gave us hope.

When Faith was diagnosed with advanced lymphoma, we went to MD Anderson Cancer Center for treatment. They offered a relatively new protocol for lymphoma that was appropriate for her condition. The care there was critical during that difficult time.
